events.text: The patient is a 39-year-old female named Renata Okonkwo. She is of Nigerian-American descent, a non-smoker, with a significant family history of breast cancer (mother diagnosed at age 42, maternal aunt with ovarian cancer). She is premenopausal, G2P2, with no prior personal history of cancer. She works as a high school biology teacher.

At age 39 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IIB (T2 N1 M0, AJCC 8th edition) INVASIVE DUCTAL CARCINOMA, NOS (MALIGNANT, PRIMARY) of the LEFT BREAST, upper outer quadrant (ICD-O site code C50.4). Histology code: 8500/3. Tumor grade: Grade 3 (Nottingham score 9/9: tubule formation 3, nuclear pleomorphism 3, mitotic count 3). Receptor status: ER negative (Allred score 0/8, 0% staining), PR negative (Allred score 0/8, 0% staining), HER2 negative (IHC 1+, FISH not performed per ASCO/CAP guidelines). Ki-67 proliferation index: 85%. Tumor size: 3.2 cm on ultrasound. Lymph node status: one clinically suspicious ipsilateral axillary lymph node identified on ultrasound. Summary stage: Regional. Laterality: Left. Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY via core needle biopsy. Tumor marker: Triple-negative (ER-/PR-/HER2-). Bilateral breast MRI showed 3.4 cm irregular enhancing mass in left breast upper outer quadrant with one abnormal-appearing left axillary lymph node. No evidence of contralateral disease or distant metastasis on staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan.

At age 39, the patient had a pathology result from a left breast core needle biopsy (ultrasound-guided, 4 cores, 14-gauge). Gross description: Four cores of tan-white tissue each measuring approximately 1.5 cm in length. Microscopic findings: Invasive ductal carcinoma, no special type (NST), grade 3. Tumor cells arranged in solid sheets and nests with pushing borders. Marked nuclear pleomorphism, prominent nucleoli, brisk mitotic activity (>20 mitoses per 10 high-power fields). Central necrosis present. No lymphovascular invasion identified on core biopsy. Immunohistochemistry: ER negative (0%), PR negative (0%), HER2 1+ by IHC (negative). Ki-67: 85%. CK5/6 positive (consistent with basal-like subtype). p53 aberrant staining pattern (diffuse strong positivity, approximately 80% of cells, suggesting TP53 gain-of-function mutation). EGFR positive (2+). Pathologic diagnosis: Invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, triple-negative, basal-like phenotype.

At age 39, the patient had a bilateral diagnostic mammogram and targeted ultrasound of the left breast and axilla. Mammogram findings: 3.1 cm irregular high-density mass with spiculated margins in the left upper outer quadrant at 10 o'clock, 4 cm from the nipple. No microcalcifications. No contralateral abnormality. Ultrasound findings: 3.2 x 2.8 x 2.1 cm irregular hypoechoic mass with angular margins and posterior acoustic shadowing at left 10 o'clock. Color Doppler shows increased internal vascularity. One left axillary lymph node measuring 2.1 cm with cortical thickening (>3 mm) and loss of fatty hilum. Fine needle aspiration of the axillary lymph node performed. BI-RADS category 5: Highly suggestive of malignancy. Cancer status: Cancer present. No distant metastatic disease identified.

At age 39, the patient had a staging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast. Chest: 3.4 cm left breast mass confirmed. No pulmonary nodules. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Left axillary lymphadenopathy with one node measuring 2.2 cm. Abdomen: Liver, spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys unremarkable. No hepatic lesions. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Pelvis: Uterus and ovaries unremarkable. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. Impression: Stage IIB left breast cancer with ipsilateral axillary lymph node involvement. No evidence of distant metastatic disease. Cancer status: Cancer present, locoregional disease only. No metastatic sites identified.

At age 39, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for invasive ductal carcinoma, triple-negative, based on a specimen obtained from the left breast core needle biopsy. Tumor mutational burden (TMB): 9.2 mutations/Mb (intermediate). Microsatellite status: MSS (microsatellite stable). PD-L1 expression by IHC (22C3 antibody): CPS (combined positive score) = 18 (positive, threshold >=10). Actionable alterations: TP53 p.R248W gain-of-function mutation (variant allele frequency 68%, likely somatic and pathogenic). PIK3CA wild-type. PTEN loss (homozygous deletion, copy number 0). RB1 p.Q550* nonsense mutation (VAF 52%, somatic). Comutations and additional somatic findings: MYC amplification (copy number 14, high-level amplification). CCND1 wild-type. CDH1 wild-type. BRCA1 somatic p.V1687fs frameshift mutation (VAF 45%, somatic; germline status to be confirmed by germline testing). RAD51C promoter methylation detected. FANCD2 wild-type. ATM wild-type. CHEK2 wild-type. AURKA amplification (copy number 6). FGFR1 wild-type. EGFR copy number gain (copy number 4, low-level). ESR1 wild-type. AR wild-type. NOTCH1 p.P2415fs frameshift mutation (VAF 31%). Copy number alterations: gain of chromosomes 1q, 8q, 10p; loss of chromosomes 5q, 8p, 13q, 17p (consistent with TP53 loss). No clinically relevant fusions detected. Note: Germline genetic counseling recommended given family history and somatic BRCA1 finding.

At age 39, the patient had an oncology new patient consultation. The patient presented with a self-discovered left breast mass present for approximately 6 weeks. She initially delayed seeking medical attention. PMH: unremarkable. Family history as noted above is highly significant. She is premenopausal, last menstrual period 2 weeks ago. ECOG performance status: 0. Physical examination: Left breast 10 o'clock hard, immobile 3 cm+ mass. Left axillary lymphadenopathy palpable (one node, approximately 2 cm). No supraclavicular or contralateral axillary adenopathy. No skin changes, no nipple discharge. Discussion: Stage IIB triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma. Given age at diagnosis, family history, and tumor characteristics, germline BRCA testing strongly recommended prior to finalizing surgical approach. Tumor biology (TNBC, high Ki-67, high grade) favors neoadjuvant chemotherapy approach to allow assessment of pathologic response and achieve potential breast-conserving surgery. Germline counseling referral placed. Discussed pembrolizumab eligibility given PD-L1 CPS 18. Plan: Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab + dose-dense AC x 4 cycles, followed by paclitaxel + pembrolizumab x 4 cycles per KEYNOTE-522 regimen. Cancer present, no response to therapy yet (treatment naive).

At age 39, the patient had a germline genetic testing result (blood-based). Testing performed: Comprehensive hereditary breast and ovarian cancer panel (26-gene panel including BRCA1, BRCA2, PALB2, CHEK2, ATM, CDH1, PTEN, STK11, TP53, and others). Results: BRCA1 pathogenic variant detected: c.5074del (p.Cys1692ValfsTer17), heterozygous. This is a well-characterized pathogenic frameshift variant in BRCA1 (OMIM #113705). Classification: Pathogenic. All other genes on the panel: No pathogenic variants or likely pathogenic variants detected. Interpretation: Hereditary breast and ovarian cancer (HBOC) syndrome. Lifetime risk of contralateral breast cancer substantially elevated. Ovarian cancer risk approximately 44-46% lifetime. Genetic counseling recommended for first-degree relatives. Given BRCA1 carrier status, consideration of risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy after completion of childbearing should be discussed. Surgical planning: BRCA1 status favors consideration of bilateral mastectomy at time of definitive surgery.

At age 39, the patient initiated neoadjuvant systemic therapy per KEYNOTE-522 protocol: Pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks combined with dose-dense doxorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV and cyclophosphamide 600 mg/m2 IV every 2 weeks (with G-CSF support, pegfilgrastim 6 mg SC day 2 of each cycle) for 4 cycles (approximately 8 weeks). Intent: neoadjuvant/curative. Baseline ECOG PS 0. Baseline left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) 62% by echocardiogram. Pre-treatment labs: CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH all within normal limits. ANC 4,200/uL. Weight 68 kg, BSA 1.74 m2. Doxorubicin cumulative dose planned: 240 mg/m2 (total 4 cycles). Prophylactic ondansetron and dexamethasone administered. BRCA1 carrier counseling regarding future PARP inhibitor eligibility reviewed.

At age 39, during cycle 2 of dose-dense AC + pembrolizumab, the patient developed immune-related thyroiditis. She presented with fatigue, palpitations, heat intolerance, and weight loss of 3 kg over 2 weeks. TSH <0.01 mIU/L (severely suppressed), free T4 3.9 ng/dL (elevated, normal 0.8-1.8). Free T3 elevated at 7.1 pg/mL. Thyroid peroxidase antibodies (TPO Ab): positive at 312 IU/mL. Thyroglobulin antibodies: positive. Diagnosis: Pembrolizumab-induced immune thyroiditis (thyrotoxic phase), CTCAE Grade 2. Management: Pembrolizumab held for 1 cycle. Atenolol 25 mg daily initiated for symptomatic relief. No corticosteroids required. Endocrinology consulted. Patient monitored closely. Chemotherapy (AC) continued on schedule as the immune-related AE was Grade 2 and isolated to the thyroid. Patient counseled regarding expected transition to hypothyroid phase.

At age 39, following cycle 3 of neoadjuvant therapy (approximately 6 weeks into treatment), the patient developed transition to hypothyroid phase of immune thyroiditis. TSH 18.4 mIU/L (elevated), free T4 0.6 ng/dL (low). She reported fatigue, constipation, and mild cold intolerance. CTCAE Grade 2 hypothyroidism. Levothyroxine 75 mcg daily initiated. Pembrolizumab restarted (had been held one cycle). She also experienced Grade 2 alopecia (expected, non-immune-related), Grade 1 nausea managed with ondansetron, and Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy (fingertip tingling). ANC nadir 1,100/uL after cycle 2 (no febrile neutropenia). LVEF reassessment after 2 cycles of doxorubicin: 59% (stable, no significant decline).

At age 39, after completion of 4 cycles of dose-dense AC + pembrolizumab (approximately 8 weeks into neoadjuvant therapy), the patient had a restaging MRI of the left breast. Findings: Previously identified 3.4 cm mass in the left upper outer quadrant now measures 2.1 cm (38% reduction in longest diameter). The mass remains irregular with areas of internal necrosis and heterogeneous enhancement. Left axillary lymph node: cortical thickening has decreased, the dominant node now measures 1.3 cm (previously 2.2 cm). Response assessment: Partial response per RECIST 1.1. Cancer present, responding to therapy. No new lesions. Assessment supports continuation of neoadjuvant treatment with paclitaxel-based therapy.

At age 39, following completion of 4 cycles of dose-dense AC, the patient transitioned to the second phase of neoadjuvant therapy: Paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 IV weekly for 12 weeks plus pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks (4 doses). Premedication: Diphenhydramine, ranitidine, dexamethasone prior to each weekly paclitaxel infusion. G-CSF not required with weekly paclitaxel. Levothyroxine 75 mcg daily continued. TSH monitoring every 4 weeks. ECOG PS 1 at start of paclitaxel phase due to residual fatigue from prior cycles. Baseline peripheral neuropathy grade 1 noted from AC phase.

At age 39, during weeks 6-9 of the paclitaxel phase of neoadjuvant therapy, the patient developed progressive peripheral neuropathy. Symptoms: bilateral fingertip and toe numbness and tingling, Grade 2 (symptomatic, limiting instrumental ADLs). No motor weakness. She also experienced Grade 2 myalgia and arthralgia, particularly in the hands and feet. Paclitaxel dose reduced by 20% (to 64 mg/m2) per institutional protocol after Grade 2 neuropathy. Patient continued to report Grade 2 fatigue. TSH on levothyroxine 75 mcg: 4.2 mIU/L (within normal range). No further immune-related adverse events during the paclitaxel + pembrolizumab phase. The patient completed all 12 weeks of paclitaxel and 4 doses of pembrolizumab in the neoadjuvant setting despite the dose reduction.

At age 39, after completing the full neoadjuvant regimen (dose-dense AC x4 + paclitaxel x12 weeks, all with pembrolizumab), a pre-surgical restaging MRI of the left breast and axilla was performed. Findings: Residual left breast mass in the upper outer quadrant now measures 1.4 x 1.1 cm (previously 2.1 cm after AC; baseline 3.4 cm). The mass demonstrates heterogeneous enhancement with a non-mass enhancement pattern, suggesting areas of treatment effect intermixed with residual viable tumor. Left axillary lymph node: the previously enlarged lymph node now measures 0.9 cm with partial resolution of cortical thickening, though still mildly abnormal in morphology. No new ipsilateral or contralateral breast lesions. No evidence of distant metastasis. Response assessment: Near-complete but not complete radiographic response. Cancer status: Cancer likely present (residual disease). Pre-surgical imaging does not meet criteria for complete radiographic response (ypT0/Tis ypN0).

At age 39, the patient underwent definitive breast surgery. Given BRCA1 carrier status, patient elected bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy with immediate tissue expander placement for subsequent reconstruction. Procedure: Left modified radical mastectomy (nipple-sparing) with left sentinel lymph node biopsy and axillary lymph node dissection (given pre-treatment positive axillary node), plus right prophylactic nipple-sparing mastectomy with immediate bilateral tissue expander placement. Operative findings: Left breast specimen with area of residual induration in the upper outer quadrant. No gross skin or nipple involvement. Left sentinel lymph node identified (1 node). Left axillary dissection: 14 lymph nodes removed. Operating time: 4.5 hours. Estimated blood loss: 200 mL. No intraoperative complications. Specimen sent to pathology for yp staging. Patient tolerated procedure well.

At age 39, the patient had a surgical pathology result from bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy and left axillary lymph node dissection. LEFT BREAST SPECIMEN: Gross: 420g mastectomy specimen. On sectioning, a 1.3 x 0.9 x 0.8 cm firm gray-white area in the upper outer quadrant. Microscopic: Residual invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, with extensive treatment effect (approximately 60-70% tumor necrosis, fibrosis, and foamy macrophage infiltrates). Residual viable tumor nests present within areas of treatment-associated fibrosis. No lymphovascular invasion identified in the post-treatment specimen. Margins: All margins negative (closest margin 8 mm, posterior). Nipple: No involvement. Skin: No involvement. LEFT SENTINEL LYMPH NODE (1 node): Isolated tumor cells detected (0.15 mm cluster, cytokeratin AE1/AE3 positive). Classification: ypN0(i+). LEFT AXILLARY LYMPH NODES (14 nodes removed): 2 of 14 nodes with metastatic carcinoma (largest metastatic deposit 4 mm and 2 mm). No extranodal extension. Classification: ypN1. RIGHT BREAST (prophylactic): No carcinoma identified. No DCIS. No atypical hyperplasia. FINAL PATHOLOGIC STAGE (ypTNM): ypT1b ypN1 M0. RCB (Residual Cancer Burden) score calculated: RCB class II (intermediate residual disease). NOT a pathologic complete response (non-pCR). ER/PR/HER2 on residual tumor: ER negative, PR negative, HER2 1+ (unchanged receptor status).

At age 39, the patient had a post-operative oncology visit at 4 weeks after surgery. She was recovering well from bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy. Drains had been removed. Tissue expanders in place. Wound healing without complications. Peripheral neuropathy had improved to Grade 1 (residual fingertip tingling bilaterally). Thyroid function on levothyroxine 75 mcg: TSH 3.1 mIU/L (stable). Pathologic staging reviewed: ypT1b ypN1 M0, RCB class II. Non-pCR status confirmed. Discussion of adjuvant therapy options: Per CREATE-X and current guidelines, capecitabine recommended for non-pCR TNBC. Per KEYNOTE-522, pembrolizumab maintenance recommended to complete 1 year total (approximately 9 doses of pembrolizumab remaining after neoadjuvant course). Plan: Adjuvant capecitabine 1000 mg/m2 BID days 1-14 every 21 days x 6-8 cycles, combined with pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 21 days to complete 1 year total duration. Olaparib discussed: given BRCA1 germline mutation, OlympiA trial data reviewed; however, patient and oncologist elected to proceed with capecitabine + pembrolizumab first, with olaparib as a subsequent option if needed. Fertility counseling completed (patient had previously banked embryos prior to chemotherapy). Risk-reducing BSO discussed with gynecologic oncology to be scheduled after adjuvant treatment. Cancer present (residual), partial response to neoadjuvant therapy.

At age 39, the patient initiated adjuvant systemic therapy: Capecitabine 1000 mg/m2 orally twice daily, days 1-14 of each 21-day cycle, for planned 8 cycles (approximately 6 months), combined with pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 21 days to complete a total of 1 year of pembrolizumab (approximately 9 additional doses in the adjuvant setting). Total pembrolizumab duration: ~17 doses across neoadjuvant and adjuvant phases. Levothyroxine 75 mcg daily continued. Pre-treatment BSA: 1.71 m2. Capecitabine dose: 1710 mg BID (rounded per pharmacy). Patient counseled extensively about hand-foot syndrome (HFS) prevention: urea-based cream, avoid tight footwear, keep hands/feet moisturized. Baseline CBC, CMP, LFTs within normal limits. Patient started therapy 6 weeks post-surgery.

At age 39, during cycles 2-3 of adjuvant capecitabine + pembrolizumab, the patient developed palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot syndrome). Symptoms: bilateral palmar and plantar erythema, tenderness, and painful blistering on both feet (ball of foot and heels). Assessed as Grade 2-3 HFS. Capecitabine held for 7 days, then dose reduced by 25% (to 1250 mg BID). Patient also developed Grade 1 diarrhea (2-3 loose stools per day above baseline) managed with loperamide PRN. Pembrolizumab continued without modification. At cycle 4 follow-up, HFS had improved to Grade 1 on the reduced capecitabine dose. Patient continued on reduced dose thereafter. No colitis or other immune-related adverse events with continued pembrolizumab. Immune-mediated hepatitis monitoring (LFTs): ALT 52 U/L (Grade 1 elevation, likely capecitabine-related); resolved by cycle 5.

At age 39 (approximately 6 months after surgery, midway through adjuvant therapy), the patient had surveillance imaging with CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast. Findings: No evidence of locoregional recurrence. Bilateral tissue expanders in place. No pulmonary nodules or pleural effusions. No hepatic lesions. No mediastinal, hilar, or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. No bone lesions (cortical integrity intact). Impression: No evidence of distant metastatic disease. Cancer status: No cancer identified. Responding/in remission on adjuvant therapy.

At age 39-40, the patient completed 8 cycles of adjuvant capecitabine (on reduced dose after cycle 3) and is continuing pembrolizumab maintenance toward 1-year total. Assessment: Tolerating therapy reasonably well. Ongoing Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy (slowly improving). Grade 1 residual HFS on capecitabine reduced dose. Fatigue Grade 1. Thyroid function stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg (TSH 2.8 mIU/L). She reports significant emotional distress related to cancer diagnosis at a young age and BRCA1 carrier status. Referred to oncology social work and genetic counseling for ongoing BRCA-related discussions. Capecitabine completed after 8 cycles. Pembrolizumab maintenance ongoing (2 doses remaining to complete 1 year). Discussed risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) to be scheduled after completing pembrolizumab. No evidence of recurrence. ECOG PS 1. Cancer status: No cancer present, patient in remission.

At age 40, during the final cycle of pembrolizumab maintenance (dose 17 of 17), the patient developed immune-related pneumonitis. She presented with progressive dyspnea on exertion, dry cough, and low-grade fever over 1 week. Chest CT showed bilateral ground-glass opacities predominantly in the lower lobes with a patchy distribution, consistent with Grade 2 immune pneumonitis (CTCAE Grade 2: symptomatic, medical intervention indicated, limiting instrumental ADLs). SpO2 95% on room air. Pembrolizumab held (this was the final planned dose; held permanently given Grade 2 pneumonitis and near-completion of planned course). Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day (60 mg daily) initiated. Pulmonology consulted. Bronchoscopy with BAL: Lymphocytic alveolitis (CD4:CD8 ratio 0.8), no infection identified. BAL cultures negative. Patient responded well to corticosteroids. Prednisone tapered over 6 weeks. Dyspnea resolved, cough improved. Repeat chest CT at 4 weeks showed near-complete resolution of ground-glass opacities. This represented the final dose of pembrolizumab; the patient had received 1 full year of pembrolizumab as planned across neoadjuvant and adjuvant phases.

At age 40, the patient completed all planned therapy (neoadjuvant pembrolizumab + dose-dense AC + paclitaxel, surgery, adjuvant capecitabine x8 cycles, pembrolizumab x17 doses total over 1 year). Post-treatment assessment: Bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy site healing well. Tissue expanders exchanged for silicone implants by plastic surgery (stage 2 reconstruction). Peripheral neuropathy: Grade 1, stable. Immune thyroiditis: well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg. Pneumonitis: resolved, no residual symptoms. Prednisone taper completed. TSH 3.6 mIU/L. ECOG PS 1. Discussing transition to surveillance. Scheduled for risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy with gynecologic oncology. Referral to high-risk program for continued annual breast MRI (right chest wall/skin) and BRCA1 management. Cancer status: No evidence of disease. Patient counseled on signs of recurrence, surveillance schedule (oncology visits every 3-6 months x 3 years, then annually).

At age 40, the patient underwent risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) via laparoscopic approach, given confirmed BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant. Procedure: Laparoscopic bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. Operative findings: Grossly normal bilateral fallopian tubes and ovaries. No gross evidence of peritoneal disease. Peritoneal washings obtained. Uterus was not removed per patient preference. The patient remained premenopausal at time of surgery (confirmed by pre-operative hormone levels: FSH 7 IU/L, estradiol 110 pg/mL). Post-operative course uneventful. Specimens submitted for pathology.

At age 40, the patient had a pathology result from bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy specimens. Protocol: SEE-FIM (Sectioning and Extensively Examining the Fimbriated End) protocol applied to both fallopian tubes. RIGHT FALLOPIAN TUBE AND OVARY: Fallopian tube: No serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC). No dysplasia. Ovary: Normal ovarian parenchyma with multiple corpus lutea and follicles. No evidence of malignancy. LEFT FALLOPIAN TUBE AND OVARY: Fallopian tube: No serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC). No dysplasia. Ovary: Normal ovarian parenchyma. No evidence of malignancy. PERITONEAL WASHINGS: No malignant cells identified. FINAL DIAGNOSIS: No malignancy identified in bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy specimens. Findings consistent with normal premenopausal ovarian and tubal tissue.

At age 40, approximately 6 months after completing all cancer treatment, the patient had a routine oncology surveillance visit. She reports surgical menopause symptoms following RRSO: hot flashes (moderate, 6-8 per day), night sweats, mood changes, and vaginal dryness. Declined hormone replacement therapy given breast cancer history (TNBC). Referred to menopause clinic and integrative oncology for non-hormonal management (venlafaxine for hot flashes, vaginal moisturizers). Peripheral neuropathy: Grade 1 persisting but not worsening. Thyroid function: TSH 2.4 mIU/L on levothyroxine 75 mcg. ECOG PS 1. Imaging: Surveillance chest CT performed. No evidence of recurrence or metastatic disease. She is engaged with genetic counseling for family members. Psychological support ongoing. Cancer status: No evidence of disease. Patient tolerating post-treatment period with manageable late effects.

At age 40 (12 months post-surgery, approximately 6 months post-completion of all therapy), the patient had a surveillance CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis. Findings: Bilateral reconstructed breasts with implants in place. No evidence of locoregional recurrence in the chest wall or axilla. No pulmonary nodules. No pleural effusions. Prior ground-glass opacities (pembrolizumab pneumonitis) completely resolved. Liver, spleen, adrenal glands, and kidneys unremarkable. No hepatic lesions. No retroperitoneal or pelvic lymphadenopathy. Pelvis: Post-surgical changes from bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. No adnexal masses. No ascites. Impression: No evidence of recurrent or metastatic breast cancer. Cancer status: No cancer identified. Patient in remission.

At age 40-41, the patient had a 18-month post-surgical surveillance oncology visit. She continues to do well clinically. Peripheral neuropathy has improved to trace/Grade 0-1. Surgical menopause symptoms have improved with venlafaxine 75 mg daily and behavioral interventions. Levothyroxine dose unchanged (TSH 2.9 mIU/L). She has returned to full-time work as a biology teacher. ECOG PS 0-1. She reports excellent quality of life and engagement in survivorship care. Surveillance mammogram not applicable (bilateral mastectomy). Annual chest wall/skin surveillance MRI of bilateral reconstructed breasts performed at 12 months post-surgery: no evidence of recurrence. CA 27.29 and CA 15-3 tumor markers not routinely monitored per ASCO guidelines (not recommended for TNBC surveillance). Discussion: Patient remains at elevated risk for recurrence (TNBC, non-pCR, RCB II, BRCA1 carrier). Continued close surveillance. Patient counseled on BRCA1 implications for family members (two children; genetic testing offered at age 18-25). Cancer status: No evidence of disease. Patient in complete clinical remission.

documents.text:
MRI BREAST WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST — RESTAGING, LEFT BREAST AND LEFT AXILLA

EXAMINATION: MRI of the bilateral breasts with and without gadolinium-based contrast enhancement, dedicated left breast and left axilla protocol.

INDICATION: 39-year-old premenopausal female with known left breast triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma (ER-/PR-/HER2-, grade 3, Ki-67 85%), stage IIB at presentation (T2 N1 M0). Currently undergoing neoadjuvant chemotherapy per KEYNOTE-522 protocol: dose-dense doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide combined with pembrolizumab, 4 cycles now completed. Restaging MRI requested to assess treatment response prior to transition to weekly paclitaxel phase. Baseline MRI at time of diagnosis demonstrated a 3.4 cm irregular enhancing mass in the left upper outer quadrant with one morphologically abnormal left axillary lymph node.

TECHNIQUE: Multiplanar, multi-sequence MRI of the bilateral breasts was performed on a 3.0 Tesla scanner utilizing a dedicated bilateral breast surface coil. Sequences obtained include axial T1-weighted, axial T2-weighted fat-suppressed, diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with ADC mapping, and dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE) series with pre- and multiple sequential post-contrast subtraction acquisitions following intravenous administration of gadobutrol (Gadavist) 0.1 mmol/kg. MIP reconstructions and subtraction images reviewed. Background parenchymal enhancement: moderate (heterogeneous). Fibroglandular tissue: heterogeneous fibroglandular tissue bilaterally. No contraindications to gadolinium administration noted. Comparison: Bilateral breast MRI performed at diagnosis.


FINDINGS:

LEFT BREAST:

Primary Lesion: There is a residual irregular mass in the left upper outer quadrant at approximately the 10 o'clock position, 4 cm from the nipple, corresponding to the known site of malignancy. The lesion currently measures approximately 2.1 x 1.8 x 1.7 cm in greatest axial, transverse, and craniocaudal dimensions, respectively. This represents appreciable interval reduction compared to the baseline MRI measurement of 3.4 x 3.0 x 2.8 cm. The lesion continues to demonstrate irregular, spiculated margins with a persistent rim-enhancing pattern and internal heterogeneity on DCE sequences. The kinetic enhancement profile demonstrates a mixed plateau/washout pattern within the residual enhancing component, which retains features of malignant kinetics, most conspicuous on the delayed-phase subtraction images. However, the area of internal non-enhancement has expanded relative to the baseline study, with a larger central region showing absent or minimal enhancement interspersed among the residual enhancing tumor nests. This pattern is consistent with treatment-related necrosis and fibrosis replacing portions of the tumor volume. On T2-weighted fat-suppressed sequences, there is a T2-hypointense core with peripheral T2 signal heterogeneity, suggesting a mixture of fibrotic treatment effect and viable residual tumor. DWI demonstrates persistently restricted diffusion within the enhancing components of the lesion (ADC approximately 0.82 x 10⁻³ mm²/s), while the centrally necrotic non-enhancing areas show facilitated diffusion consistent with liquefactive necrosis. No new satellite lesions are identified in the left breast. No additional foci of suspicious enhancement elsewhere in the left breast.

Nipple-Areolar Complex: The left nipple-areolar complex does not demonstrate abnormal enhancement. No retraction or skin thickening is identified.

Skin and Chest Wall: No suspicious skin thickening, skin enhancement, or skin involvement by the primary lesion. The pectoralis major and minor muscles demonstrate normal signal intensity without evidence of direct chest wall invasion.

Left Breast Lymphatics: No suspicious intramammary lymph nodes.

RIGHT BREAST:

The right breast demonstrates heterogeneous fibroglandular tissue with moderate background parenchymal enhancement, expected for a premenopausal woman. No mass, architectural distortion, or suspicious focus of enhancement is identified in the right breast. No suspicious axillary lymph nodes on the right.

LEFT AXILLA:

The morphologically abnormal left axillary lymph node identified on the baseline study is again visualized. The dominant node now measures approximately 1.3 cm in short-axis diameter (previously 2.2 cm). There has been appreciable interval reduction in the degree of cortical thickening; cortex now measures approximately 4 mm (previously >7 mm). The fatty hilum remains indistinct, though the degree of hilar effacement has decreased compared to baseline. The remaining nodal architecture appears less distorted. One additional small left axillary lymph node measuring 0.7 cm in short axis is identified; this node demonstrates preserved fatty hilum and thin cortex, appearing reactive rather than involved. No matted nodal conglomerates. No level II or level III axillary adenopathy. No infraclavicular or supraclavicular adenopathy identified on the imaged portions.

INTERNAL MAMMARY AND MEDIASTINUM: No internal mammary lymph nodes identified. The imaged mediastinal structures appear unremarkable.

OTHER FINDINGS: No bone marrow signal abnormalities are identified in the visualized sternum, clavicles, or ribs to suggest osseous involvement. No pleural effusion.


IMPRESSION:

1. LEFT BREAST PRIMARY LESION — PARTIAL RESPONSE: The known left upper outer quadrant triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma demonstrates meaningful interval reduction in size from 3.4 cm at baseline to approximately 2.1 cm in greatest dimension following 4 cycles of dose-dense doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide and pembrolizumab. The mass retains viable-appearing enhancing components with malignant kinetic features, but a substantially larger proportion of the lesion is now occupied by centrally necrotic, non-enhancing treatment effect compared to baseline. Overall morphology and kinetics remain consistent with residual viable malignancy intermixed with treatment-induced necrosis and fibrosis. The lesion has not resolved and does not meet criteria for complete radiographic response.

2. LEFT AXILLARY LYMPH NODE — INTERVAL IMPROVEMENT: The previously enlarged morphologically abnormal left axillary lymph node shows interval reduction in size and decrease in cortical thickening, reflecting a treatment response at the nodal level. However, the node remains abnormal in morphology with persistent cortical thickening and loss of fatty hilum, and cannot be characterized as radiographically normal at this time. Continued monitoring warranted.

3. NO NEW DISEASE: No new suspicious enhancing foci identified in either breast. No new lymphadenopathy. No contralateral breast lesion.

4. RIGHT BREAST: Unremarkable. No suspicious findings.

Overall, the imaging picture is consistent with a meaningful but incomplete treatment response to the completed dose-dense AC and pembrolizumab phase of neoadjuvant therapy, with residual enhancing disease present in the left breast and left axilla.

MRI BILATERAL BREASTS AND LEFT AXILLA — PRE-SURGICAL RESTAGING

EXAMINATION TYPE: Bilateral breast MRI with and without IV gadolinium contrast (gadobenate dimeglumine 0.1 mmol/kg). Dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE) sequences, T1-weighted pre- and post-contrast, T2-weighted fat-saturated, and diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI/ADC mapping) obtained in the axial and sagittal planes. Subtraction images reviewed. Computer-aided detection (CAD) post-processing applied.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 39-year-old premenopausal female with known left breast invasive ductal carcinoma, triple-negative, grade 3, BRCA1 germline carrier (c.5074del). Patient has completed full neoadjuvant systemic therapy: four cycles of dose-dense Adriamycin/CTX concurrently with pembro, followed by twelve weeks of weekly Taxol with pembro every three weeks. Prior breast MRI performed after completion of the AC component demonstrated partial interval reduction in the primary lesion from 3.4 cm at baseline to 2.1 cm at the mid-treatment time point. This examination is obtained to assess response to full neoadjuvant course and to guide surgical planning prior to planned bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy.

COMPARISON: Bilateral breast MRI performed after four cycles of dose-dense AC plus pembro (approximately eight weeks into treatment). Diagnostic bilateral mammogram and ultrasound obtained at initial staging.

---

FINDINGS:

LEFT BREAST:

There is a residual area of enhancement in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast at approximately the 10 o'clock position, 3 to 4 cm from the nipple. This corresponds to the site of the previously biopsy-proven invasive ductal carcinoma. The enhancing abnormality has substantially decreased in size compared to both the mid-treatment MRI and the pre-treatment baseline imaging.

Current morphology: The residual lesion now measures approximately 1.4 x 1.1 x 0.9 cm in its three orthogonal dimensions (previously 2.1 x 1.7 x 1.4 cm at the mid-treatment time point, and 3.4 x 3.0 x 2.8 cm at baseline). The previously defined discrete irregular mass with well-demarcated internal architecture has substantially evolved in character. There is no longer a dominant, contiguous, well-circumscribed mass. Instead, the residual enhancement follows a fragmented, non-mass enhancement pattern, with a clumped to heterogeneous internal distribution, occupying a geographic footprint slightly larger than the measurable enhancement alone would suggest. This morphologic shift — from a solid mass toward a non-mass enhancement pattern — is a recognized imaging feature of treatment-related change and is consistent with progressive tumor response intermixed with residual viable foci.

On T2-weighted sequences, the area of prior mass has been replaced by heterogeneous signal: there are scattered foci of intermediate-to-low T2 signal (consistent with residual tumor nests or dense fibrosis) embedded within areas of elevated T2 signal (edema, treatment-associated necrosis, liquefied debris). No dominant T2-hypointense mass comparable to the pre-treatment lesion is now identifiable.

Diffusion-weighted imaging: Patchy restricted diffusion is present within the enhancing footprint, with corresponding ADC values in the range of approximately 0.78 x 10^-3 mm²/s at the lowest focal area, suggesting residual cellularity is present, though overall the diffusional restriction is substantially diminished compared to what would be expected of the pre-treatment tumor. Broad areas of facilitated diffusion are interspersed, consistent with areas of tumor replacement by necrosis, fibrosis, and macrophage infiltration.

Kinetic analysis of the enhancing foci: Several punctate foci within the non-mass enhancement region demonstrate persistent kinetic curves. A subset shows plateau or washout kinetics on delayed phases, which in the context of known malignancy is interpreted as favoring residual viable tumor over purely treatment-related enhancement. The overall peak enhancement and wash-in slope are diminished relative to the pre-treatment examination, consistent with substantial reduction in tumor vascularity following cytotoxic therapy.

No new satellite lesions or additional foci of suspicious enhancement are identified in the left breast outside of the known treatment field. No evidence of nipple or areolar involvement. No overlying skin thickening or retraction. The posterior margin of the residual enhancing area appears at least 8 to 10 mm from the chest wall fascia by visual estimation, though exact margins will require pathologic assessment.

No suspicious enhancement or discrete mass identified in the left lower inner quadrant, lower outer quadrant, or retroareolar region.

RIGHT BREAST:

No suspicious mass lesion, asymmetric non-mass enhancement, or abnormal lymph node identified in the right breast. No evidence of occult malignancy in the contralateral breast. Background parenchymal enhancement is moderate and symmetric to a limited degree given the asymmetric status. No skin or nipple changes. No axillary adenopathy in the right axilla.

LEFT AXILLA:

The previously enlarged left axillary lymph node, which measured 2.2 cm on the staging CT and had demonstrated significant cortical thickening and loss of fatty hilum on the pre-treatment ultrasound, has shown appreciable interval change. The dominant node at level I now measures approximately 0.9 x 0.6 cm. There is partial reconstitution of a central fatty hilum, though it remains attenuated and incompletely preserved. The cortex, while substantially thinned compared to the pre-treatment state, retains mild residual irregularity and slightly eccentric thickening at the superior pole, measuring approximately 3 mm at its thickest point. This node has not returned to fully normal morphologic criteria and should not be considered definitively uninvolved by imaging alone.

No second-tier or high axillary (level II/III) lymph nodes with abnormal morphology are identified. No internal mammary chain lymphadenopathy is seen.

CHEST WALL AND SKIN:

No chest wall invasion. No abnormal enhancement of the pectoralis major muscle or serratus anterior. Surgical clip artifact noted from prior core needle biopsy site in the left upper outer quadrant, serving as concordant localization. No skin enhancement. No dermal lymphatic pattern.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Marked interval reduction in the left breast primary lesion compared to both baseline and mid-treatment imaging. The residual enhancing abnormality in the left upper outer quadrant now spans approximately 1.4 cm in its longest dimension, representing a substantial decrease from the 3.4 cm baseline lesion and the 2.1 cm measurement obtained after the AC component of neoadjuvant therapy. The overall reduction in size from baseline exceeds 55% of the original longest diameter.

2. The residual lesion has transitioned from a discrete irregular mass to a fragmented non-mass enhancement pattern, a morphologic appearance that reflects significant treatment-induced change. However, the presence of residual areas of clumped enhancement with washout kinetics and focal restricted diffusion is concerning for ongoing viable tumor within areas of treatment-related fibrosis and necrosis. This appearance is not consistent with complete pathologic response.

3. Left axillary lymph node: the dominant node has decreased substantially in size to 0.9 cm with partial recovery of the fatty hilum. Mild residual cortical irregularity persists and the node cannot be declared morphologically normal. While the imaging trajectory is favorable and suggests significant treatment response, the possibility of residual microscopic nodal disease cannot be excluded on the basis of this examination.

4. No new lesions in the left breast, right breast, or right axilla. No evidence of distant or contralateral disease within the field of view.

5. Taken in totality, the imaging findings are most consistent with a near-complete but not complete radiographic response to the full neoadjuvant regimen. The presence of residual non-mass enhancement with concerning kinetic features and restricted diffusion is not compatible with a radiographically complete response. Pathologic assessment of the surgical specimen will be necessary to determine the true extent of residual disease.

6. These findings support proceeding with planned definitive surgery. Clip is present and will serve as a fiducial marker for the pathologist.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Okonkwo is a 39-year-old BRCA1 carrier with stage IIB triple-negative, grade 3 invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast, now 4 weeks post bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy and left ALND. She has received 8 doses of pembro in the neoadjuvant setting. Final pathology confirms ypT1b ypN1 M0 disease with RCB class II residual disease — non-pCR. The non-pCR result is clinically significant and warrants intensification of the adjuvant treatment plan beyond standard of care for pCR patients:

1. ADJUVANT THERAPY — Xeloda:
Per CREATE-X trial data (Masuda et al., NEJM 2017), patients with HER2-negative breast cancer (including TNBC) who do not achieve pCR after neoadjuvant chemotherapy derive significant survival benefit from adjuvant Xeloda. In the TNBC subgroup, this benefit was particularly pronounced (5-year DFS improvement of approximately 13%). I discussed these data in detail with Ms. Okonkwo today, including the absolute benefit, expected toxicities, and the rationale for proceeding.

Plan: Xeloda 1000 mg/m2 orally twice daily, days 1 through 14 of each 21-day cycle, for 6-8 cycles (we will plan for 8 cycles). BSA: 1.71 m2 (weight 66.2 kg today). Pharmacy-calculated dose: 1710 mg PO BID (rounded per institutional rounding table). To start in approximately 2 weeks (6 weeks post-surgery), pending wound healing confirmation and adequate hematologic recovery.

Hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, HFS) prevention counseling provided extensively today:
  - Urea-based cream (e.g., Eucerin or Udderly Smooth) applied to palms and soles twice daily starting day 1 of treatment
  - Avoid constrictive footwear, prolonged standing, and friction-causing activities
  - Keep hands and feet cool and moisturized
  - Avoid hot water exposure (dishes, baths)
  - Report Grade 1 symptoms early so we can intervene before progression to Grade 2-3
  - Written HFS handout provided

Other anticipated cape toxicities reviewed: diarrhea (loperamide PRN per protocol), nausea (ondansetron PRN), fatigue, mucositis. Instructed patient to hold cape and call if Grade 2 or greater toxicity develops.

DHPD deficiency: Patient does not have known DPYD variants (not tested prior to initiation given CREATE-X data and prior treatment; will order DPYD pharmacogenomic testing before the first dose to screen for severe cape toxicity risk).

2. ADJUVANT pembro CONTINUATION (KEYNOTE-522):
Per KEYNOTE-522 protocol, pembro is administered for a total of approximately 1 year from initiation (17 doses total across neoadjuvant and adjuvant phases). Ms. Okonkwo received 8 doses in the neoadjuvant setting. She therefore has approximately 9 additional doses of Keytruda 200 mg IV every 21 days remaining in the adjuvant setting.

Given the thyroiditis that occurred during neoadjuvant Keytruda (well-managed, no Grade 3 irAEs), she is an acceptable candidate to resume pembro in the adjuvant setting. Thyroid function is currently stable. We will restart pembro 200 mg IV every 3 weeks concurrently with Xeloda.

Monitoring plan on adjuvant Keytruda + cape:
  - CBC, CMP, LFTs every 3 weeks prior to each Keytruda dose
  - TSH every 6-8 weeks (ongoing thyroiditis monitoring)
  - Blood pressure monitoring
  - Vigilance for new immune-related adverse events (colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, adrenal insufficiency, etc.)
  - Patient educated on irAE symptoms to monitor and when to call the clinic

3. BRCA1-RELATED ADJUVANT OPTIONS (ola):
Given germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant and non-pCR after neoadjuvant platinum-free chemotherapy, the patient is potentially eligible for adjuvant ola per the OlympiA trial (Tutt et al., NEJM 2021). OlympiA demonstrated a significant improvement in invasive disease-free survival and overall survival with adjuvant Lynparza 300 mg BID for 1 year in high-risk BRCA1/2-mutated HER2-negative breast cancer patients.

After careful discussion with the patient today, we are electing to proceed with cape + pembro first, followed by consideration of ola as a subsequent or overlapping option if needed based on disease trajectory and toxicity tolerance. We discussed that Xeloda + pembro and ola have not been directly compared in the adjuvant setting and the possibility of sequential use is reasonable. This decision was made collaboratively with the patient, who is appropriately motivated to maximize survival outcomes while managing quality of life concerns.

Lynparza will be revisited at the end of adjuvant Xeloda, particularly if no recurrence has occurred and Keytruda is complete.

4. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (ONGOING):
Grade 1 bilateral fingertip paresthesia, improving since discontinuation of Taxol. No pharmacologic treatment indicated at this grade. Will continue to monitor. If neuropathy worsens or is significantly impacting quality of life, duloxetine may be considered per ASCO guidelines (limited evidence but some benefit for CIPN).

5. IMMUNE THYROIDITIS / HYPOTHYROIDISM:
Well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH 3.1 mIU/L today — no dose adjustment needed. Will continue 6-8 week TSH monitoring while on pembro. She understands to call if she develops recurrence of thyroid symptoms.

6. FERTILITY AND REPRODUCTIVE HEALTH:
Chemotherapy-induced amenorrhea present. Pre-treatment embryo cryopreservation was completed. Discussed that menstrual recovery may occur after stopping chemotherapy, though recovery is variable and not guaranteed at her age. She does not wish to pursue additional pregnancy at this time; both pregnancies are complete per her report. BRCA1 implications for her two children (ages 7 and 10) discussed — genetic testing recommended when they reach adulthood (ages 18-25). Letters for family members provided per genetics program protocol.

7. RISK-REDUCING BILATERAL SALPINGO-OOPHORECTOMY (RRSO):
Given confirmed BRCA1 carrier status, RRSO is strongly recommended per NCCN HBOC guidelines (recommended age 35-40 for BRCA1 carriers). The patient was premenopausal at the time of surgery. We will refer to gynecologic oncology now; however, given ongoing active treatment, RRSO will be scheduled after completing adjuvant systemic therapy to minimize surgical stress during treatment. Target: schedule RRSO for approximately 6-9 months from today (after completing cape and approaching or completing Keytruda adjuvant dosing).

8. SURVIVORSHIP AND PSYCHOSOCIAL:
Ms. Okonkwo reports significant emotional distress related to her diagnosis at age 39, the non-pCR result (discussed openly and honestly today), BRCA1 carrier status and implications for her children, and uncertainty about long-term prognosis. She was tearful during the discussion of the RCB class II result and the rationale for intensified adjuvant therapy. I spent additional time with her providing emotional support, honest prognostic counseling, and emphasizing that her treatment team remains committed to maximizing her long-term outcomes.

Referrals/supports in place:
  - Oncology social work (established, active)
  - Genetic counseling (active, for family cascade testing)
  - Plastic surgery (tissue expander fills on track per plastic surgery team)
  - Physical therapy for left upper extremity lymphedema surveillance (baseline circumferential measurements obtained by PT today)
  - Integrative oncology referral placed (acupuncture, mind-body medicine for anxiety/sleep)

I addressed the patient's specific question today: "Does the non-pCR mean my cancer is likely to come back?" I provided balanced, honest counseling that RCB class II represents an intermediate risk group and that the additional adjuvant therapy we are planning (Xeloda + pembro, and potentially ola) is specifically designed to reduce this residual risk. I also noted that 5-year recurrence-free survival rates in patients like her who go on to receive adjuvant cape after non-pCR are meaningfully improved compared to no additional therapy.

9. FOLLOW-UP:
- Labs (CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH) and visit in 2 weeks to confirm clearance to start Xeloda and pembro
- Keytruda infusion and cape prescription to be initiated at that 2-week visit
- DPYD testing ordered today; results pending before cycle 1
- Routine surveillance: After completing adjuvant therapy, oncology visits every 3-4 months for first 2 years, every 6 months years 3-5, then annually
- Annual chest wall and bilateral reconstructed breast MRI (no standard mammogram given bilateral mastectomy)
- No routine CT surveillance or tumor marker monitoring per ASCO TNBC survivorship guidelines

Time spent with patient today: 55 minutes, greater than 50% in counseling and coordination of care.

All four cores are examined. The histologic sections demonstrate cores of breast parenchyma extensively replaced by a highly cellular malignant epithelial neoplasm. The tumor cells are arranged predominantly in solid sheets and confluent nests with focal trabecular architecture. The neoplasm is characterized by marked nuclear pleomorphism: the nuclei are large, vesicular, and highly irregular in shape and size, with coarse chromatin and multiple prominent nucleoli (nuclear grade 3). The cytoplasm is moderate in amount and eosinophilic. Mitotic figures are abundant, including atypical mitoses, numbering greater than 20 per 10 high-power fields (40x objective). Geographic central necrosis ("comedo-type" necrosis) is present within the solid tumor nests. Extensive chronic and acute inflammatory infiltrate is noted at the periphery of tumor nests, including a notable tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) population estimated at approximately 30-40% stromal TILs.

The tumor demonstrates pushing borders with focal infiltrative growth into the surrounding fibrous stroma. The adjacent non-neoplastic breast parenchyma, where identifiable, shows normal lobular architecture. No ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) is definitively identified in the cores, though the sampling is limited by the nature of core biopsy. No lymphovascular invasion is identified in the submitted cores, though evaluation is limited by the biopsy nature of the specimen.

The overall histologic appearance is that of invasive carcinoma of no special type (NST; formerly invasive ductal carcinoma), high grade. Tubule formation is minimal/absent (score 3). Nuclear pleomorphism is marked (score 3). Mitotic rate is high (score 3). Nottingham combined histologic grade: 9/9, Grade 3.

Ms. Okonkwo has now completed 8 full cycles of adjuvant Xeloda (CREATE-X--based regimen, at dose-reduced level of approximately 1250 mg BID given prior Grade 2-3 HFS). She has responded to the dose reduction with manageable Grade 1 residual toxicity. Per our discussion today, Xeloda is now COMPLETE. She continues pembro 200 mg IV every 21 days; she has approximately 2 doses remaining to fulfill the planned 1-year total pembro duration as specified per KEYNOTE-522. pembro will be administered as scheduled. She will continue to be monitored closely for immune-related adverse events with each Keytruda dose. Surveillance imaging reviewed as above -- no evidence of recurrence or distant metastasis at this time, which is very encouraging.

I reviewed with her the residual risks given non-pCR and RCB Class II status in the setting of TNBC: the highest-risk period for recurrence in TNBC is the first 2-3 years, with visceral sites (lung, liver, CNS) most common. We discussed the importance of prompt reporting of new neurologic symptoms, persistent cough, dyspnea, bone pain, or other new symptoms.

Lynparza (Lynparza) was again discussed as an adjuvant option given BRCA1 carrier status and OlympiA trial data demonstrating disease-free survival benefit in high-risk HER2-negative early breast cancer with germline BRCA mutations. Patient and I reviewed: she completed neoadjuvant therapy and had non-pCR, she is BRCA1 germline positive, and she technically meets eligibility criteria for adjuvant Lynparza per OlympiA. However, given that she has been on an extended course of chemotherapy (including anthracycline, taxane, and Xeloda) as well as immunotherapy, and given her current toxicity burden (fatigue, neuropathy, HFS recovery), we have elected -- consistent with her preference and shared decision-making -- to complete the pembro course first and reassess before adding Lynparza. This is a reasonable approach. We will re-discuss at the next visit after she completes pembro whether she wishes to pursue adjuvant Lynparza (300 mg PO BID x 1 year) given the additional DFS benefit, weighing her fatigue, neuropathy, and quality of life concerns. The patient is aware of this option and will inform her decision over the next 4-6 weeks.

2. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY -- GRADE 1, IMPROVING
Bilateral fingertip and plantar numbness consistent with chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN), predominantly from prior Taxol exposure. Now Grade 1, improved from Grade 2 peak during weekly Taxol. No motor involvement. Reassuring trajectory. I reassured Ms. Okonkwo that ongoing improvement over 6-12 months is expected for many patients, though some residual neuropathy may persist. No pharmacologic intervention for neuropathy currently indicated (does not meet threshold for duloxetine by ASCO guidelines given Grade 1 severity). She is encouraged to continue gentle hand and foot exercises, avoid tight footwear, and continue urea-based moisturizers for the skin. Will monitor at next visit.

3. IMMUNE-MEDIATED HYPOTHYROIDISM -- STABLE ON LEVOTHYROXINE
pembro-induced immune thyroiditis with permanent hypothyroidism, well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH 2.8 mIU/L at this visit. Symptoms (mild cold intolerance) are minimal. She should continue levothyroxine indefinitely. TSH will be rechecked in approximately 3 months or sooner if symptoms change. Endocrinology has been following; she will maintain that relationship for ongoing thyroid management. No dose adjustment required at this time.

4. HAND-FOOT SYNDROME -- GRADE 1, RESOLVING AFTER Xeloda COMPLETION
HFS peaked at Grade 2-3 during cycles 2-3 of Xeloda, with painful plantar blistering, and improved to Grade 1 on the reduced dose. Now with Xeloda complete, I expect gradual further resolution over the next 2-4 weeks. She should continue consistent application of urea-based or petroleum-based emollients to palms and soles. No further Xeloda dose adjustments are relevant given completion of the planned 8-cycle course.

5. PSYCHOSOCIAL DISTRESS AND BRCA1 CARRIER STATUS
Ms. Okonkwo continues to report significant anxiety and emotional burden related to her diagnosis at a young age, BRCA1 status, and concerns about her two children. She is engaged with oncology social work and is working with an individual therapist, which I commend. We discussed that her BRCA1 c.5074del pathogenic variant is hereditary; her children have a 50% chance of having inherited it, and offer of genetic testing at age 18-25 (or earlier if planning families) was reiterated. She is encouraged to involve her siblings and parents in genetic counseling discussions as well, as first-degree relatives should be offered cascade testing.

Regarding risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO): she underwent laparoscopic RRSO (fallopian tubes and ovaries removed, uterus preserved per her preference) at age 40, with pathology demonstrating no evidence of malignancy and no STIC lesions -- an excellent result. This is expected to meaningfully reduce her lifetime ovarian cancer risk (from approximately 44-46% to <2%). She is now in surgical menopause. We discussed non-hormonal options for menopause symptoms (ongoing use of venlafaxine 75 mg for vasomotor symptoms, topical vaginal moisturizers). She remains ineligible for systemic hormone therapy given her breast cancer history.

Fertility: She had previously banked embryos prior to initiating chemotherapy; this remains available to her in the future if desired. Given surgical menopause (RRSO), natural conception is no longer possible.

6. SURVEILLANCE PLAN -- TRANSITION TO POST-TREATMENT SURVEILLANCE
Given that Xeloda is now complete and Keytruda will be completed within the next 6 weeks (2 remaining doses), we discussed transitioning to the survivorship/surveillance phase:
- Oncology follow-up: every 3-4 months for the first 2 years post-completion of therapy, then every 6 months through year 5, then annually. Given non-pCR status and BRCA1 mutation, closer follow-up is warranted in the near term.
- Annual chest wall/skin MRI of bilateral reconstructed breasts: standard for patients who have undergone mastectomy with BRCA1 mutation; this will be scheduled at 12 months post-surgery.
- CT surveillance: will continue per standard TNBC high-risk surveillance at 12-month intervals (or sooner if symptoms arise), particularly given non-pCR and RCB II.
- Tumor markers (CA 27.29, CA 15-3): not recommended per ASCO guidelines for routine surveillance in TNBC.
- Bone health: Given surgical menopause at age 40, DEXA scan recommended within 1-2 years to establish baseline bone mineral density; bisphosphonate therapy may be considered depending on result.
- High-risk program referral: Reiterated referral to our institutional hereditary breast cancer high-risk program for BRCA1-specific ongoing management, including right chest wall/skin surveillance.
- Plastic surgery: Tissue expander-to-implant exchange (stage 2 reconstruction) planned with plastic surgery once all adjuvant systemic therapy is completed. Scheduled for approximately 6-8 weeks after last Keytruda dose.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo is a 40-year-old Nigerian-American premenopausal woman (at time of diagnosis; now post-RRSO surgically menopausal) with a confirmed BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant who was diagnosed approximately 14-15 months ago with Stage IIB triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast (3.2 cm, grade 3, ER/PR/HER2-negative, Ki-67 85%, basal-like phenotype, CPS 18 by PD-L1 immunohistochemistry). She had one biopsy-confirmed left axillary lymph node at presentation.

She received neoadjuvant therapy per the KEYNOTE-522 backbone: pembro 200 mg IV q3 weeks combined with dose-dense doxo and cyclo (ddAC) every 2 weeks for 4 cycles, followed by pacli 80 mg/m2 IV weekly for 12 weeks (with a 20% dose reduction to 64 mg/m2 from approximately week 7 onward due to Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy) plus pembro every 3 weeks for an additional 4 doses. Her neoadjuvant course was complicated by pembro-induced immune thyroiditis (initial thyrotoxic phase followed by persistent hypothyroidism), for which she required pembro to be held for one cycle and was started on levothyroxine, which she continues. She also experienced grade 2-3 hand-foot syndrome during the Xeloda phase and grade 2 peripheral neuropathy during the Taxol phase.

She underwent bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy with left axillary lymph node dissection plus right prophylactic mastectomy. Surgical pathology confirmed residual invasive ductal carcinoma (ypT1b) with 2 of 14 axillary lymph nodes involved (ypN1), classifying her as RCB Class II -- not a pathologic complete response. Receptor status on residual tumor remained triple-negative.

Adjuvant therapy consisted of cape 1000 mg/m2 BID (subsequently dose-reduced to 750 mg/m2 BID after Grade 2-3 hand-foot syndrome in cycles 2-3) on days 1-14 of each 21-day cycle for 8 cycles, combined with pembro 200 mg IV every 3 weeks to complete a total of 1 year of checkpoint inhibitor therapy. During the final planned dose of Keytruda (dose 17 of 17), she developed Grade 2 immune-related pneumonitis with bilateral lower lobe ground-glass opacities on CT. Keytruda was held (and ultimately not restarted, as this was the final planned dose). She was treated with prednisone 1 mg/kg/day, tapered over 6 weeks, with excellent clinical and radiographic response. She is now approximately 4-6 weeks beyond completing the prednisone taper, and her respiratory symptoms have fully resolved.

Most recently, her stage 2 breast reconstruction was completed by plastic surgery, with exchange of bilateral tissue expanders for silicone implants. She is recovering well from that procedure.

Today she presents reporting that she feels "cautiously hopeful" and is beginning to mentally process the transition out of active treatment. She denies new symptoms of concern. She has some residual tingling in her fingertips, which is improving. She reports mild fatigue, which she attributes to recovery from both the reconstruction surgery and the prolonged course of treatment. She denies dyspnea, cough, chest pain, or any respiratory symptoms. She denies new bone pain, headaches, or neurologic changes.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. TRIPLE-NEGATIVE BREAST CANCER -- END OF PLANNED TREATMENT / TRANSITION TO SURVEILLANCE

Ms. Okonkwo has now completed all planned systemic therapy for her stage IIB TNBC. To summarize her treatment course:
   - Neoadjuvant: Keytruda + ddAC x4 cycles, then weekly Taxol (dose-reduced from cycle 7) + Keytruda x12 weeks/4 doses
   - Surgery: Bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy, left ALND (ypT1b ypN1 M0, RCB Class II)
   - Adjuvant: cape x8 cycles (dose-reduced after Grade 2-3 HFS) + pembro x9 additional doses (17 total)
   - Total Keytruda exposure: 1 full year (17 doses), as planned per KEYNOTE-522
   - Reconstruction: Bilateral tissue expanders exchanged for silicone implants
   - Risk reduction: Risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (pathology negative)

She did not achieve a pathologic complete response (RCB Class II, ypT1b ypN1), which places her in a group with modestly elevated recurrence risk, particularly in the first 2-3 years. However, she completed the adjuvant cape per CREATE-X protocol, which has been shown to improve outcomes in non-pCR TNBC. Additionally, completing a full year of Keytruda per KEYNOTE-522 is associated with improved event-free survival. We are cautiously optimistic.

I discussed with Renata today that there is no standard of care systemic therapy to offer at this time beyond surveillance. We reviewed the OlympiA data for adjuvant ola in BRCA1/2 carriers with high-risk HER2-negative breast cancer; she has completed cape and Keytruda, and the OlympiA eligibility window (initiated within 12 weeks of chemotherapy/surgery completion) has passed given her sequential adjuvant regimen. We discussed this openly, and she understands. If disease recurs, PARP inhibitor-based therapy (ola monotherapy or platinum-based regimens) would be strongly considered at that time given her BRCA1 status.

Surveillance plan:
   - Oncology visits: Every 3 months for the first year post-treatment completion, then every 4-6 months in years 2-3, then annually thereafter
   - CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: Every 6-12 months for the first 3 years given non-pCR, TNBC, and BRCA1 status (institutional surveillance protocol)
   - Annual chest wall/skin MRI of bilateral reconstructed breasts (right mastectomy site and left chest wall) beginning at 1 year post-surgery, as bilateral mastectomy does not eliminate small risk of skin/chest wall recurrence, particularly relevant for BRCA1 carriers
   - No routine CA 27.29 or CA 15-3 tumor marker monitoring per ASCO guidelines; not recommended for TNBC surveillance
   - Annual clinical breast exam (chest wall and axilla)
   - Echocardiogram ordered today (post-anthracycline surveillance)

2. pembro-INDUCED PNEUMONITIS -- RESOLVED

Grade 2 immune pneumonitis developed during dose 17 (final dose) of Keytruda. She completed prednisone 1 mg/kg/day with slow 6-week taper, now fully completed approximately 4-6 weeks ago. CT chest confirms complete radiographic resolution. She is clinically asymptomatic from a pulmonary standpoint. SpO2 98% on room air today. Pulmonology saw her during the acute event and has discharged her from active follow-up. No further corticosteroids indicated. Will monitor for any signs of recurrent pulmonary or other immune-related symptoms given the potential for persistent immune dysregulation following checkpoint inhibition. She should be counseled to report any new respiratory symptoms promptly.

3. IMMUNE-MEDIATED HYPOTHYROIDISM -- WELL-CONTROLLED

Chronic sequela of pembro-induced thyroiditis. Currently on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH 3.6 mIU/L today -- within target range. No dose adjustment needed. Will continue thyroid function monitoring every 6 months going forward, or sooner if symptomatic. Patient is aware she will likely require lifelong thyroid hormone replacement.

4. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY -- GRADE 1, IMPROVING

Residual PTX-induced peripheral neuropathy affecting bilateral fingertips and toes, now Grade 1 and improving from the Grade 2 nadir during the weekly PTX phase. No motor deficits. Reassured that PTX-induced neuropathy typically continues to improve over 6-12 months following completion of treatment, though a small degree of residual deficit may persist. No pharmacologic intervention warranted at Grade 1. Will continue to monitor. If progresses or causes functional limitation, can consider duloxetine (noting she is already on venlafaxine -- see below).

5. SURGICAL MENOPAUSE / MENOPAUSAL SYMPTOMS FOLLOWING RRSO

Ms. Okonkwo underwent risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy given BRCA1 carrier status, inducing surgical menopause. She is experiencing moderate vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes 6-8/day, night sweats) and vaginal dryness. Hormone replacement therapy is not appropriate given her history of TNBC. Initiating venlafaxine (Effexor) 37.5 mg daily with planned uptitration to 75 mg daily at 2 weeks, which has good evidence for vasomotor symptom management in breast cancer patients. Referred to menopause clinic for comprehensive management. Vaginal moisturizers (non-hormonal, e.g., Replens) recommended. Discussed pelvic floor physical therapy referral. Bone density (DEXA scan) ordered given surgical menopause at age 40 and prior corticosteroid exposure; will assess need for bisphosphonate therapy at follow-up.

6. BRCA1 CARRIER STATUS -- ONGOING MANAGEMENT

Risk-reducing BSO completed (pathology negative bilaterally). Breast surveillance ongoing per above plan (annual chest wall MRI). No further cancer-risk-reducing surgical options relevant to her present anatomy (bilateral mastectomy complete, BSO complete). Genetic counseling remains active; patient is engaged in discussions about first-degree relatives. Her two children (presumably prepubertal/adolescent given patient age) should be counseled regarding BRCA1 testing when they reach adulthood (recommended at age 18-25). Patient is in contact with the hereditary cancer program.

7. BILATERAL BREAST RECONSTRUCTION -- COMPLETED

Stage 2 reconstruction performed by plastic surgery with exchange of tissue expanders for silicone implants. Excellent cosmetic result. No signs of infection or early capsular contracture. Follow-up with plastic surgery for ongoing reconstruction care. Patient satisfied with the result and reports this has contributed positively to her body image and psychological recovery.

8. PSYCHOSOCIAL / SURVIVORSHIP

Ms. Okonkwo carries a significant emotional burden: young-onset TNBC diagnosis, BRCA1 carrier status, surgical menopause at 40, prolonged and demanding treatment course, ongoing fears of recurrence, and impact on her role as a mother and teacher. She is engaged with oncology social work and has been evaluated by psychology. I had an extended conversation today about survivorship, the meaning of NED (no evidence of disease), and realistic expectations around recurrence risk. She was tearful at points but expressed relief at having completed treatment. I validated her experience and reinforced that surveillance is active and evidence-based.

Referral to a TNBC survivorship support group placed. Will provide contact information for the Young Survival Coalition (YSC) given her age at diagnosis.

Return to work: She has expressed desire to return to full-time teaching. We discussed a gradual return, monitoring for fatigue. If fatigue remains problematic, referral to integrative oncology for assessment.

FERTILITY: Previously banked embryos prior to neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Fertility status is now moot given bilateral oophorectomy; she was counseled regarding this prior to RRSO and understood its implications.

SUMMARY

Renata Okonkwo is a 40-year-old BRCA1-positive woman who has completed all planned therapy for Stage IIB TNBC and is currently with no evidence of disease. She has navigated an intensive, year-long treatment course with significant toxicities including immune thyroiditis, peripheral neuropathy, hand-foot syndrome, and pneumonitis, all of which have been managed and have largely resolved or improved. She transitions today to an active surveillance program. Long-term goals include close oncologic surveillance for recurrence, management of surgical menopause, ongoing BRCA1-related risk management, and psychosocial support as she returns to her life as a survivor.

Next visit: In 3 months.

No loss of PAX8 expression. Cell proliferation index by Ki-67 is low (less than 10% of epithelial cells). No secretory cell outgrowths (SCOUTs) identified meeting criteria for concern. No serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC). No evidence of invasive carcinoma. No tubal intraepithelial neoplasia.

Ovary: Normal premenopausal ovarian parenchyma. Multiple primordial, primary, and secondary follicles identified in the cortical stroma. One corpus luteum with central fibrotic zone. Ovarian stroma unremarkable without stromal hyperplasia, thecomatosis, or hilar cell proliferations. No endometriosis. No surface epithelial inclusions with atypia. No granulosa cell proliferations. No evidence of malignancy.

SPECIMEN B - LEFT FALLOPIAN TUBE AND OVARY:
Fallopian tube (SEE-FIM protocol): Serial sections of the fimbriated end and entire fallopian tube examined. The tubal epithelium is normal in appearance with ciliated columnar and secretory (PAX8-positive) cells without cytologic atypia. p53 immunohistochemistry demonstrates wild-type staining pattern (scattered low-level positivity). Ki-67 proliferation index low (less than 10% of tubal epithelial cells). No secretory cell outgrowths (SCOUTs) of significance. No serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC). No tubal intraepithelial neoplasia. No evidence of invasive carcinoma.

Ovary: Normal premenopausal ovarian parenchyma. Multiple small follicles in various stages of development. Ovarian cortex and medulla without significant pathologic change. No surface implants. No endometriosis. No malignancy identified.

SPECIMEN C - PERITONEAL WASHINGS:
Cytospin preparation and cell block reviewed. Mesothelial cells present in small groups with normal cytomorphology. Inflammatory cells including lymphocytes and rare macrophages. No atypical epithelial cells. No malignant cells identified.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ANCILLARY STUDIES:

Immunohistochemistry (performed on fimbriated end sections, Specimens A and B):
  - p53 (DO-7): Wild-type staining pattern bilaterally. No aberrant diffuse strong nuclear overexpression. No complete absence (null pattern). Negative for p53 aberrant staining indicative of TP53 mutation in the tubal epithelium.
  - Ki-67 (MIB-1): Less than 10% nuclear staining in tubal epithelial cells bilaterally. Low proliferation index.
  - PAX8: Diffuse nuclear positivity in secretory tubal epithelial cells, as expected. No loss of PAX8.

Note: p53 immunohistochemistry is routinely performed in BRCA1/2 carriers undergoing RRSO to evaluate for occult serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC). Aberrant p53 (diffuse strong overexpression or null staining) would prompt further investigation. In this case, wild-type p53 staining is reassuring.

Cytology (Specimen C): No malignant cells identified. See cytopathology report [accession number on file] for detailed interpretation.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
FINAL PATHOLOGIC DIAGNOSIS:

A. RIGHT FALLOPIAN TUBE AND OVARY (laparoscopic excision):
   - No serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC)
   - No tubal dysplasia or intraepithelial neoplasia
   - Normal premenopausal ovarian parenchyma; no evidence of malignancy
   - Wild-type p53 immunostaining pattern in tubal epithelium
   - SEE-FIM protocol applied; entire fimbriated end examined

B. LEFT FALLOPIAN TUBE AND OVARY (laparoscopic excision):
   - No serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC)
   - No tubal dysplasia or intraepithelial neoplasia
   - Normal premenopausal ovarian parenchyma; no evidence of malignancy
   - Wild-type p53 immunostaining pattern in tubal epithelium
   - SEE-FIM protocol applied; entire fimbriated end examined

C. PERITONEAL WASHINGS:
   - Negative for malignant cells
   - Reactive mesothelial cells and inflammatory cells only

OVERALL SUMMARY: Risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy in a BRCA1 germline carrier. No occult malignancy, STIC, or premalignant lesion identified in either specimen. Findings are consistent with normal premenopausal ovarian and tubal tissue. SEE-FIM protocol satisfied for both fallopian tubes.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
PATHOLOGIC STAGING: Not applicable (no malignancy identified).

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
COMMENT:
The SEE-FIM (Sectioning and Extensively Examining the Fimbriated End) protocol was applied to both fallopian tube specimens per standard of care for BRCA1/2 germline carriers undergoing risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy. This protocol involves complete submission of the fimbriated end in 2 mm serial sections, which is the site of highest risk for occult serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) in BRCA1 mutation carriers. p53 immunohistochemistry was performed as part of this protocol. No STIC or occult carcinoma was identified. These findings are consistent with a negative RRSO and should be interpreted in the clinical context of ongoing surveillance.

Despite a non-pCR (RCB class II, ypT1b ypN1 M0) — which carries a higher recurrence risk than pCR, particularly in the first 3-5 years — she has remained disease-free to date. Her BRCA1 carrier status remains an ongoing consideration both for recurrence risk in the chest wall/skin and for the previously addressed ovarian cancer risk (now mitigated by completed RRSO). I had an extensive discussion with her today about her overall prognosis, acknowledging both the favorable trajectory thus far and the importance of maintaining close surveillance.
   - Continue surveillance visits every 3-4 months through year 3, then every 6 months through year 5, then annually.
   - Next surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to be performed at 24 months post-surgery (approximately 6 months from today's visit) unless new symptoms arise.
