SOMATC ALTERATIONS — CLINICALLY RELEVANT / POTENTIALLY ACTIONABLE
------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. TP53 p.R248W (c.742C>T)
   Gene: TP53 (17p13.1)
   Variant Class: Missense — gain-of-function
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 68%
   Copy Number Context: 17p LOH (loss of heterozygosity) confirmed; consistent with biallelic inactivation via mutation + allelic loss
   Interpretation: The p.R248W substitution is among the most frequently observed TP53 hotspot mutations in human cancer, classified as a well-characterized gain-of-function (GOF) variant. At the biochemical level, this mutant p53 protein retains DNA-binding capability but acquires oncogenic properties including transactivation of proliferation-associated genes, interaction with p63/p73 to antagonize their tumor-suppressive functions, and promotion of genomic instability. In breast cancer, TP53 p.R248W is strongly enriched in the basal-like/TNBC subtype and is associated with high-grade histology, high Ki-67, aggressive clinical behavior, and BRCA1-associated tumors. The aberrant diffuse p53 IHC staining pattern observed on core biopsy is consistent with this gain-of-function variant. The high VAF (68%) and co-occurrence with 17p LOH support clonal dominance and biallelic TP53 loss. Classification: Pathogenic, somatic (likely). Germline TP53 is not suspected given absence of Li-Fraumeni-pattern family history and the presence of a strong BRCA-phenotype family history; however, germline TP53 testing is reflexed through the comprehensive hereditary panel ordered concurrently.
   Therapeutic Relevance: TP53 mutations are not directly targeted by approved therapies in breast cancer at this time. This finding confirms the high-genomic-instability, basal-like biology of the tumor. GOF TP53 mutations may confer relative resistance to certain DNA-damaging agents through dysregulation of apoptotic pathways; however, this does not preclude benefit from standard neoadjuvant chemotherapy regimens used in TNBC.

2. BRCA1 p.C1692Vfs*17 (c.5074del) — SOMATIC (germline status pending)
   Gene: BRCA1 (17q21.31)
   Variant Class: Frameshift deletion — loss of function
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 45%
   Copy Number Context: 17q LOH present; consistent with biallelic BRCA1 inactivation
   Interpretation: A single-nucleotide deletion in exon 17 of BRCA1 results in a frameshift at codon 1692, introducing a premature stop codon 17 amino acids downstream (p.Cys1692ValfsTer17). This leads to production of a severely truncated BRCA1 protein lacking the critical BRCT domain, abrogating its role in homologous recombination-mediated DNA double-strand break repair, replication fork protection, and cell cycle checkpoint signaling. The VAF of 45% in a tumor with 65% estimated tumor purity is consistent with a heterozygous somatic event; however, given this patient's strong family history (mother with breast cancer diagnosed at age 42; maternal aunt with ovarian cancer), germline origin of this variant cannot be excluded. IMPORTANT NOTE: Germline genetic testing has been requisitioned concurrently (comprehensive hereditary breast and ovarian cancer multi-gene panel from peripheral blood); results are pending. If confirmed germline, this would establish a diagnosis of Hereditary Breast and Ovarian Cancer (HBOC) syndrome with substantial implications for surgical management, systemic therapy selection, and family cascade testing. Co-occurrence of BRCA1 somatic mutation, RAD51C promoter methylation, and a positive HRD signature score strongly supports functional homologous recombination deficiency in this tumor, regardless of germline status.
   Therapeutic Relevance: BRCA1 loss-of-function is a predictive biomarker for sensitivity to PARP inhibitors (olaparib, talazoparib) and platinum-based chemotherapy. In the germline-confirmed setting, the OlympiA trial demonstrated significant improvement in invasive disease-free survival with adjuvant olaparib in high-risk, HER2-negative breast cancer patients with germline BRCA1/2 mutations following (neo)adjuvant chemotherapy. This finding should be integrated with final germline results to guide adjuvant therapy planning.

3. PTEN — Homozygous Deletion
   Gene: PTEN (10q23.31)
   Variant Class: Copy number loss — homozygous deletion
   Copy Number: 0 (complete biallelic loss)
   VAF: N/A (copy number event)
   Interpretation: Complete homozygous deletion of PTEN, a critical tumor suppressor encoding a lipid phosphatase that negatively regulates the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling axis. PTEN loss leads to constitutive PI3K pathway activation, promoting cell survival, proliferation, and angiogenesis. PTEN deletions are found in approximately 25-35% of TNBC cases and are associated with more aggressive tumor biology and potentially reduced sensitivity to certain therapies. Concurrent PIK3CA wild-type status (confirmed, see below) indicates PI3K pathway activation via PTEN loss rather than PIK3CA mutation, which has distinct implications for potential PI3K inhibitor selection.
   Therapeutic Relevance: PTEN loss is a predictive biomarker under investigation for AKT inhibitors (capivasertib, ipatasertib) in TNBC. The LOTUS and IPATunity130 trials have investigated ipatasertib + paclitaxel in TNBC with PI3K pathway alterations. This finding may be relevant for future clinical trial enrollment if the patient progresses.

4. RB1 p.Q550* (c.1648C>T)
   Gene: RB1 (13q14.2)
   Variant Class: Nonsense — loss of function
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 52%
   Copy Number Context: 13q LOH confirmed
   Interpretation: A C>T transition at codon 550 introduces a premature stop codon (p.Gln550*) in the RB1 gene, encoding the retinoblastoma protein (pRb). pRb is a master regulator of the G1/S cell cycle checkpoint; its inactivation leads to unrestrained entry into S phase and genomic instability. The high VAF (52%) in the context of 13q LOH supports biallelic inactivation (mutation + loss of the remaining allele), consistent with complete functional loss of RB1. RB1 loss is enriched in triple-negative breast cancer and is associated with specific response patterns: in vitro and clinical data suggest RB1-null tumors may have reduced sensitivity to CDK4/6 inhibitors, but enhanced sensitivity to certain taxane-based and DNA-damaging regimens. Co-occurrence of RB1 loss with TP53 mutation and BRCA1 loss defines an extreme genomic instability phenotype.
   Therapeutic Relevance: RB1 loss predicts lack of benefit from CDK4/6 inhibition (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) — not currently indicated in TNBC but relevant for context. May be associated with sensitivity to aurora kinase inhibitors, given co-occurring AURKA amplification (see below).

------------------------------------------------------------------------
ADDITIONAL SOMATIC FINDINGS
------------------------------------------------------------------------

5. MYC — High-Level Amplification
   Gene: MYC (8q24.21)
   Copy Number: 14 copies (reference diploid = 2; ratio to chromosome 8 centromere: 7.0)
   Interpretation: High-level amplification of MYC, encoding the transcription factor c-Myc, a central regulator of cell cycle progression, ribosome biogenesis, metabolic reprogramming, and apoptosis resistance. MYC amplification (8q24 gain) is present in approximately 15-20% of TNBC and is often co-selected with concurrent PTEN loss and TP53 mutation, forming a highly oncogenic gene network. High-level MYC amplification is associated with elevated Ki-67 (consistent with the 85% observed in this tumor), high histologic grade, and overall aggressive clinical behavior. Concurrent gains of chromosome 1q and 8q (see copy number section) are frequent co-occurring events.
   Therapeutic Relevance: No direct MYC-targeted therapies are currently approved. BET bromodomain inhibitors (e.g., BETi) targeting MYC transcription are in early clinical development. This finding further supports a high-proliferative, genomically unstable tumor phenotype.

6. NOTCH1 p.P2415fs (c.7241del)
   Gene: NOTCH1 (9q34.3)
   Variant Class: Frameshift deletion — likely loss of function
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 31%
   Interpretation: A frameshift deletion in the NOTCH1 PEST domain (C-terminal regulatory region), introducing a premature stop codon. PEST domain mutations in NOTCH1 have context-dependent effects: in T-cell leukemia, PEST domain mutations are activating (loss of proteasomal degradation signal); however, in solid tumors, including TNBC, the functional consequences are less well characterized. The sub-clonal VAF of 31% suggests this mutation may be present in a subset of tumor cells. NOTCH pathway dysregulation has been described in TNBC and may contribute to cancer stem cell maintenance and treatment resistance, but there are no approved NOTCH-directed therapies in breast cancer at present.
   Therapeutic Relevance: Investigational at this time; no approved indication.

7. AURKA — Copy Number Gain (Amplification)
   Gene: AURKA (20q13.2)
   Copy Number: 6 copies (moderate amplification)
   Interpretation: Copy number gain of AURKA, encoding Aurora kinase A, a serine/threonine kinase critical for mitotic spindle assembly, centrosome maturation, and chromosomal segregation. AURKA amplification or overexpression is observed in 10-20% of TNBC and contributes to chromosomal instability (CIN), aneuploidy, and resistance to taxane-based therapy through perturbation of spindle assembly checkpoint function. Co-occurrence with RB1 loss is biologically synergistic in promoting aberrant mitosis.
   Therapeutic Relevance: Aurora kinase A inhibitors (alisertib) are in clinical trials for TNBC. No approved indication currently.

8. EGFR — Copy Number Gain (Low-Level)
   Gene: EGFR (7p11.2)
   Copy Number: 4 copies (low-level gain; no high-level amplification)
   Interpretation: Low-level copy number gain of EGFR, consistent with the IHC 2+ EGFR staining observed on core biopsy. High-level EGFR amplification (not present here) or activating point mutations (not detected in this tumor) are the canonical criteria for EGFR-directed therapy; low-level copy gain alone does not predict benefit from EGFR-targeted agents in breast cancer.
   Therapeutic Relevance: Not actionable at current copy number level in breast cancer. No activating EGFR mutations detected.

GERMLINE IMPLICATIONS / HEREDITARY TESTING NOTE
------------------------------------------------------------------------
A somatic BRCA1 frameshift variant (p.C1692Vfs*17, c.5074del) has been identified in this tumor at a VAF of 45%. In the context of a strong family history of breast and ovarian cancer (mother diagnosed at age 42, maternal aunt with ovarian cancer), this somatic finding raises the clinical suspicion for a germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (with the somatic event representing the second hit leading to biallelic inactivation). Concurrent matched normal sequencing (peripheral blood buffy coat) was performed to assess germline origin; based on matched normal analysis, the c.5074del variant was ALSO DETECTED in the germline comparator sample at the expected heterozygous allele frequency (approximately 48%), strongly suggesting germline origin.

IMPORTANT: Formal germline classification requires validation through a CLIA-certified germline genetic testing laboratory using a dedicated germline-focused assay (e.g., comprehensive hereditary BRCA/multi-gene panel from blood). A reflex germline test has been ordered. Genetic counseling is strongly recommended prior to disclosure of germline results. This somatic report cannot be used as a substitute for germline genetic testing.

Additional germline-relevant findings from this somatic panel:
  - ATM: No pathogenic variants detected (somatic or apparent germline)
  - CHEK2: No pathogenic variants detected
  - PALB2: No pathogenic variants detected
  - TP53: The somatic p.R248W variant is not identified in the germline comparator at a frequency consistent with germline heterozygosity; this variant is classified as somatic.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERPRETIVE SUMMARY
------------------------------------------------------------------------
This comprehensive genomic profile from a grade 3, triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast in a 39-year-old woman reveals a highly complex genomic landscape characterized by:

1. A functional homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) phenotype driven by concurrent BRCA1 frameshift mutation (likely germline; formal germline testing pending), RAD51C promoter methylation, and an elevated HRD composite scar score (53). This HRD phenotype is the most therapeutically consequential finding in this report.

2. Biallelic TP53 inactivation via GOF p.R248W mutation and 17p LOH, consistent with the basal-like/TNBC subtype and high-grade histology.

3. PI3K/AKT pathway activation via complete homozygous PTEN deletion (PIK3CA wild-type), potentially targetable with AKT inhibitors in the investigational setting or future lines of therapy.

4. Cell cycle checkpoint disruption via biallelic RB1 loss, co-occurring with AURKA amplification, driving mitotic instability.

5. High-level MYC amplification and broad chromosomal instability (gains 1q, 8q; losses 5q, 8p, 13q, 17p) reflecting an aggressive, highly proliferative tumor with multiple co-occurring driver events.

6. Positive PD-L1 expression (CPS 18) supporting immunotherapy eligibility in the neoadjuvant and potentially adjuvant settings.

7. Intermediate TMB (9.2 mut/Mb); MSS status.

8. No druggable fusions detected; KRAS, BRAF, ERBB2, and PIK3CA are wild-type.

Taken together, the genomic profile is most consistent with the basal-like 1 (BL1) TNBC subtype (high proliferation, DNA damage response pathway enrichment, BRCA1/HRR deficiency). This profile predicts relative sensitivity to platinum-containing regimens and PARP inhibitors (given HRD/BRCA1 deficiency), as well as sensitivity to immune checkpoint inhibition (given PD-L1 CPS 18). Standard anthracycline/taxane-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy regimens are appropriate, and integration of pembrolizumab per KEYNOTE-522 is strongly supported by the PD-L1 CPS.

She was referred by her breast surgeon for discussion of neoadjuvant systemic therapy options and overall treatment planning.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo is a previously healthy 39-year-old woman who self-discovered a firm left breast mass approximately six weeks ago while performing a self-breast examination. By her report, she initially attributed the mass to a benign cyst and delayed seeking medical evaluation for several weeks. When the mass did not resolve and seemed to enlarge, she presented to her primary care physician, who performed a clinical breast exam and immediately referred her for diagnostic mammography and ultrasound.

Diagnostic bilateral mammogram demonstrated a 3.1 cm irregular high-density spiculated mass in the left upper outer quadrant at the 10 o'clock position, approximately 4 cm from the nipple, with no associated microcalcifications. Targeted left breast and axillary ultrasound confirmed a 3.2 x 2.8 x 2.1 cm irregular hypoechoic mass with angular margins, posterior acoustic shadowing, and increased internal vascularity on Doppler imaging. One left axillary lymph node measuring 2.1 cm with cortical thickening greater than 3 mm and loss of the normal fatty hilum was identified and was noted to be highly suspicious for nodal metastasis. The mammogram and ultrasound were assigned BI-RADS 5 (highly suggestive of malignancy).

Ultrasound-guided core needle biopsy of the left breast mass (14-gauge, 4 cores) was performed and demonstrated invasive ductal carcinoma, no special type, Grade 3 (Nottingham score 9/9), with high nuclear pleomorphism, brisk mitotic activity (>20 mitoses per 10 HPF), and central necrosis. Immunohistochemistry revealed ER-negative (Allred score 0/8, 0% staining), PR-negative (Allred score 0/8, 0% staining), and HER2 1+ by IHC (negative, FISH not performed per ASCO/CAP guidelines). Ki-67 proliferation index was markedly elevated at 85%. CK5/6 was positive, consistent with a basal-like phenotype. p53 showed diffuse strong aberrant staining in approximately 80% of cells, raising concern for a TP53 gain-of-function mutation. EGFR was positive at 2+ intensity. Fine needle aspiration of the suspicious left axillary lymph node was performed and was cytologically positive for metastatic carcinoma.

Staging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast confirmed the left breast primary and left axillary adenopathy (dominant node 2.2 cm), with no pulmonary nodules, hepatic lesions, retroperitoneal or pelvic lymphadenopathy, or other sites of distant metastasis identified. Bilateral breast MRI demonstrated a 3.4 cm irregular enhancing mass in the left upper outer quadrant and a morphologically abnormal left axillary lymph node, with no evidence of contralateral disease. No bone metastases were identified on available imaging.

Next-generation sequencing (NGS) was performed on the core biopsy specimen. Key findings include: TP53 p.R248W gain-of-function mutation (VAF 68%), PTEN homozygous deletion (copy number 0), RB1 p.Q550* nonsense mutation (VAF 52%), BRCA1 somatic frameshift mutation (p.V1687fs, VAF 45%) — germline status to be confirmed by germline testing — MYC high-level amplification (copy number 14), AURKA amplification (copy number 6), EGFR low-level copy number gain, RAD51C promoter methylation, and NOTCH1 frameshift mutation. TMB was 9.2 mutations/Mb (intermediate). Microsatellite status: MSS. PD-L1 by IHC (22C3 antibody): CPS 18 (positive, threshold >=10). PIK3CA was wild-type. No actionable fusions detected.

Given the identification of a somatic BRCA1 frameshift mutation on tumor NGS, as well as the patient's striking family history (mother diagnosed with breast cancer at age 42, maternal aunt with ovarian cancer), germline genetic testing and counseling were initiated. Formal referral to genetic counseling has been placed and germline BRCA testing is pending at this visit.

Ms. Okonkwo presents today to discuss her diagnosis, staging, pathology, and the recommended treatment approach in full. She is accompanied by her husband and states she has been researching her diagnosis online and is eager to understand her options. She is emotionally distressed but engaged and asking thoughtful questions. She specifically asked about whether she is eligible for "the immunotherapy" she read about for triple-negative breast cancer.

LABORATORY DATA

Recent pre-treatment labs (within the past 1-2 weeks, reviewed from outside records and confirmed in our system):
- CBC: WBC 7,800/uL, hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, platelets 278,000/uL, ANC 4,200/uL.
- Comprehensive metabolic panel: sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.0 mEq/L, creatinine 0.82 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL, glucose 91 mg/dL. Total bilirubin 0.5 mg/dL, AST 22 U/L, ALT 18 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 64 U/L, albumin 4.3 g/dL. All values within normal limits.
- TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (within normal range). Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal). (Baseline thyroid function documented given planned pembro.)
- LDH: 172 U/L (upper limit of normal; not definitively elevated).
- Echocardiogram to assess baseline LVEF pending (ordered today, required prior to doxo-based chemotherapy).

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Bilateral diagnostic mammogram and targeted left breast/axillary ultrasound: 3.1-3.2 cm irregular spiculated/hypoechoic mass, left upper outer quadrant, 10 o'clock, highly suspicious for malignancy. Left axillary lymph node 2.1 cm, cortically thickened with loss of fatty hilum, cytologically confirmed positive. BI-RADS 5.

Bilateral breast MRI: 3.4 cm irregular enhancing mass, left upper outer quadrant, morphologically abnormal left axillary lymph node. No contralateral disease. No additional ipsilateral satellite lesions.

Staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast: Left breast primary and left axillary adenopathy (2.2 cm) confirmed. No pulmonary nodules, hepatic lesions, retroperitoneal or pelvic adenopathy, or other sites of distant metastatic disease.

Overall: Disease appears locoregional, Stage IIB (T2 N1 M0).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Renata Okonkwo is a 39-year-old premenopausal woman with newly diagnosed Stage IIB (T2 N1 M0, AJCC 8th edition) left breast invasive ductal carcinoma, Grade 3, triple-negative subtype (ER-/PR-/HER2-), basal-like phenotype, Ki-67 85%. Her tumor biology is aggressive: high mitotic rate, central necrosis, p53 aberrant expression consistent with TP53 gain-of-function mutation, PTEN loss, RB1 mutation, MYC amplification on NGS. PD-L1 CPS is 18 (positive), making her eligible for pembro-based therapy. She has an intermediate TMB (9.2 mutations/Mb) and is MSS. She has a somatic BRCA1 frameshift mutation on tumor NGS, and germline testing is urgently pending.

1. TREATMENT STRATEGY — NEOADJUVANT APPROACH:

I strongly recommend neoadjuvant systemic therapy prior to surgery, for the following reasons:

a) Tumor biology (TNBC, high Ki-67, grade 3) is particularly suited to neoadjuvant treatment, as response to chemotherapy in the breast allows assessment of pathologic response at surgery, which carries important prognostic and therapeutic implications. A pathologic complete response (pCR — defined as no residual invasive disease in the breast or lymph nodes, ypT0/Tis ypN0) in TNBC is associated with markedly improved event-free and overall survival.

b) Neoadjuvant therapy may allow tumor downstaging, potentially increasing the likelihood of achieving breast-conserving surgery (lumpectomy), though we discussed that her BRCA1 germline testing results (pending) may independently influence her surgical decision-making regarding the contralateral breast.

c) PD-L1 CPS 18 makes her eligible for Keytruda (Keytruda) under the KEYNOTE-522 regimen, which demonstrated a statistically significant improvement in pathologic complete response rates and event-free survival in early-stage TNBC when pembro was added to standard chemotherapy.

Recommended regimen: Keytruda 200 mg IV every 3 weeks + dose-dense Adriamycin (Adriamycin) 60 mg/m2 IV + cyclo 600 mg/m2 IV every 2 weeks (with G-CSF support: pegfilgrastim 6 mg SC on day 2 of each cycle) for 4 cycles (~8 weeks), followed by PTX (Taxol) 80 mg/m2 IV weekly for 12 weeks + pembro 200 mg IV every 3 weeks for 4 additional doses.

Total neoadjuvant duration: approximately 20 weeks (5 months).

Following completion of neoadjuvant therapy, the patient will proceed to definitive breast surgery (type to be determined by breast surgery, pending BRCA1 germline result and patient preference), followed by adjuvant pembro to complete a total of 1 year of pembro.

2. Keytruda — IMMUNE-RELATED ADVERSE EVENT COUNSELING:

I spent considerable time educating Ms. Okonkwo and her husband about immune-related adverse events (irAEs) associated with pembro, including but not limited to:
- Immune-mediated pneumonitis (cough, dyspnea)
- Immune-mediated colitis (diarrhea, abdominal pain)
- Immune-mediated hepatitis (elevated liver enzymes)
- Immune-mediated endocrinopathies, particularly thyroiditis and hypophysitis (fatigue, palpitations, headaches)
- Immune-mediated dermatitis (rash, pruritus)

She was counseled to report any new symptoms promptly and that many irAEs require corticosteroid management and/or Keytruda dose modification or discontinuation. Given that she has no prior history of autoimmune disease, thyroid disease, or inflammatory bowel disease, she does not have specific contraindications to Keytruda. Baseline thyroid function documented (TSH 2.1 mIU/L, normal). Baseline hepatic function normal. Baseline CBC normal.

3. Adriamycin (ADRIAMYCIN) — CARDIOTOXICITY MONITORING:

Baseline echocardiogram ordered today to assess LVEF prior to initiation of Adriamycin. Planned cumulative doxo dose is 240 mg/m2 (4 cycles x 60 mg/m2), which is well below the traditional cumulative cardiotoxicity threshold (~450-550 mg/m2). Serial LVEF assessment will be performed per protocol.

4. G-CSF SUPPORT:

Pegfilgrastim will be used with each cycle of dose-dense AC to mitigate febrile neutropenia risk. The accelerated 2-week schedule of dose-dense AC requires mandatory growth factor support.

5. GERMLINE BRCA TESTING — URGENT:

Given the highly significant family history (mother with breast cancer at age 42, maternal aunt with ovarian cancer) and the somatic BRCA1 frameshift mutation identified on tumor NGS, I strongly recommended urgent germline genetic testing. Formal referral to genetic counseling has been placed. Results are expected within 2-3 weeks. Germline BRCA1 status will have important implications for:
- Surgical planning (e.g., consideration of bilateral mastectomy vs. unilateral mastectomy or lumpectomy)
- Future risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy (after family planning is complete)
- Eligibility for future PARP inhibitor therapy (Lynparza [Lynparza] under OlympiA trial data in the adjuvant setting for BRCA-mutant early breast cancer)
- Risk counseling for first-degree relatives including her two children

6. ADJUVANT THERAPY PLANNING (DISCUSSED IN ANTICIPATION):

For patients who do not achieve pCR after neoadjuvant therapy, adjuvant Xeloda (Xeloda) per CREATE-X trial data provides an overall survival benefit in TNBC. Additionally, per KEYNOTE-522 protocol, Keytruda is continued in the adjuvant setting to complete 1 full year of total therapy. These options were briefly introduced today to set expectations, with the understanding that final adjuvant decisions will be made at the time of post-surgical pathology review.

7. FERTILITY:

Ms. Okonkwo is premenopausal. She reports that embryo cryopreservation was completed prior to this visit with assistance from reproductive endocrinology. She does not desire additional pregnancies at this time. No further acute fertility interventions are required prior to initiating chemotherapy. She was advised that chemotherapy may result in temporary or permanent amenorrhea, and the risk of ovarian insufficiency increases with age and cumulative gonadotoxic exposure.

8. FOLLOW-UP AND LOGISTICS:

- Echocardiogram: ordered, to be completed prior to cycle 1.
- Labs (CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH): to be repeated at each chemotherapy visit.
- Germline genetic counseling appointment: scheduled (pending).
- Chemotherapy start: planned for approximately 7-10 days from today, pending LVEF results and patient readiness.
- Restaging MRI of the left breast will be performed after completion of the 4 cycles of dose-dense AC (mid-neoadjuvant response assessment).
- Pre-surgical restaging imaging (MRI left breast/axilla) will be performed after completion of the full neoadjuvant regimen.
- Patient to call oncology nurse line with any fever (temperature >100.4°F), unusual symptoms, or concerns.

I spent approximately 90 minutes with Ms. Okonkwo and her husband, discussing the diagnosis, staging, treatment rationale, risks, benefits, and alternatives. She demonstrated understanding and expressed a desire to proceed with the recommended treatment plan. She asked excellent questions regarding the rationale for adding Keytruda and the possibility of achieving a complete response. She was provided with written materials, a clinic summary, and contact information for the oncology team. She verbalized agreement with the proposed plan.

Electronic orders placed: echocardiogram, repeat baseline labs, premedication regimens, genetic counseling referral, chemotherapy order set initiated.

PATHOGENIC VARIANT IDENTIFIED:

Gene: BRCA1 (NM_007294.4)
Variant: c.5074del (p.Cys1692ValfsTer17)
Zygosity: Heterozygous
Classification: PATHOGENIC

Variant Description:
This single-nucleotide deletion within exon 17 of BRCA1 results in a frameshift beginning at codon 1692, with introduction of a premature termination codon 17 amino acids downstream (p.Cys1692ValfsTer17). The predicted consequence is production of a truncated, non-functional BRCA1 protein and/or nonsense-mediated mRNA decay, leading to loss of BRCA1 function. This variant has been documented in multiple independent families with hereditary breast and ovarian cancer (HBOC) syndrome and is well-represented in the ClinVar database (ClinVar Accession SCV000xxxxx) as pathogenic, with review status of multiple submitters, no conflicts. The variant is also catalogued in the Breast Cancer Information Core (BIC) database as a disease-causing frameshift mutation.

The variant was confirmed by bidirectional Sanger sequencing of the relevant amplicon.

Allele frequency in population databases: Absent from gnomAD v3.1 (0/251,292 alleles) and from the ExAC database, consistent with a rare, disease-associated germline variant rather than a benign polymorphism.

Sanger Confirmation: CONFIRMED (bidirectional Sanger sequencing performed on independent DNA aliquot)

Classification: Variant of Uncertain Significance (Class 3)
Clinical significance: At this time, this variant does not meet criteria for pathogenic or likely pathogenic classification. MUTYH-associated polyposis (MAP) is an autosomal recessive condition; a single heterozygous MUTYH variant is not expected to significantly increase cancer risk in the absence of a second pathogenic variant on the other allele. No second pathogenic MUTYH variant detected on this panel. Clinical significance of this heterozygous VUS alone is uncertain. Reclassification may occur as additional evidence accumulates; this laboratory will provide variant reclassification notification per standard policy.

---

INTERPRETATION

A PATHOGENIC germline variant in BRCA1 is identified in this patient: c.5074del (p.Cys1692ValfsTer17), heterozygous.

This result is diagnostic of Hereditary Breast and Ovarian Cancer (HBOC) Syndrome (OMIM #604370). Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant carriers face substantially elevated lifetime risks of breast cancer, contralateral breast cancer, and epithelial ovarian cancer (including fallopian tube and primary peritoneal carcinomas) compared to the general population. Estimated BRCA1 carrier lifetime risks based on current literature include:

-- Breast cancer (cumulative to age 80): approximately 72% (95% CI 65-79%)
-- Ovarian cancer (cumulative to age 80): approximately 44-46%
-- Contralateral breast cancer: substantially elevated above background (10-year risk estimated 20-30% following first breast cancer diagnosis)
-- Pancreatic cancer: modestly elevated
-- Melanoma: potentially modestly elevated

The identification of a somatic BRCA1 frameshift mutation (p.V1687fs, VAF 45%) on the patient's tumor NGS report, distinct from but in the same gene as the germline variant, is consistent with somatic loss of heterozygosity (LOH) at the BRCA1 locus in the tumor -- a phenomenon expected when the remaining wild-type allele is somatically inactivated. This pattern of biallelic BRCA1 inactivation (one germline allele, one somatic allele) in the tumor is consistent with BRCA1 as a classic tumor suppressor gene and further corroborates the pathogenicity of the germline variant.

Additionally, RAD51C promoter methylation detected on tumor NGS represents a somatic epigenetic event and does not indicate a germline RAD51C alteration. Germline RAD51C sequencing on this panel is negative.

Note on TP53: The somatic TP53 p.R248W gain-of-function mutation identified on tumor NGS was NOT detected in germline blood DNA. This confirms somatic origin of the TP53 mutation. Li-Fraumeni syndrome due to germline TP53 is excluded.
